title: Transforms, transitions and animations

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Transforms, transitions and animations; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Transforms, transitions and animations
sources.claim: Transitions interpolate animatable property values when computed values change, using declared duration, delay, and easing.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Transforms, transitions and animations
sources.claim: Transitions interpolate animatable property values when computed values change, using declared duration, delay, and easing.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Transitions interpolate animatable property values when computed values change, using declared duration, delay, and easing.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A disclosure icon rotates briefly when aria-expanded changes while the content state updates immediately. Your goal is to implement purposeful state motion and explain its start, end and interruption behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Flexbox and Grid; Typography, color and visual treatment; Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements; Stacking contexts and z-order. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Transitions interpolate animatable property values when computed values change, using declared duration, delay, and easing. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Display does not generally interpolate like opacity, layout properties can be expensive, and motion must not hide state or block interaction. This draft therefore rejects: transition: all is the safest way to animate every visual change consistently.

includes: Animate composite-friendly properties for feedback without making content inaccessible or disorienting.
Implement purposeful state motion and explain its start, end and interruption behavior.
excludes: Display does not generally interpolate like opacity, layout properties can be expensive, and motion must not hide state or block interaction.

outcomes: Implement purposeful state motion and explain its start, end and interruption behavior.

examples.title: Transforms, transitions and animations: concrete case
examples.situation: A disclosure icon rotates briefly when aria-expanded changes while the content state updates immediately.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A disclosure icon rotates briefly when aria-expanded changes while the content state updates immediately.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Transition only deliberate properties with measured timing; avoid transition:all and keep state understandable without motion.
examples.result: The result follows because Transition only deliberate properties with measured timing; avoid transition:all and keep state understandable without motion.

examples.title: Transforms, transitions and animations: boundary case
examples.situation: Display does not generally interpolate like opacity, layout properties can be expensive, and motion must not hide state or block interaction.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Display does not generally interpolate like opacity, layout properties can be expensive, and motion must not hide state or block interaction.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: transition: all is the safest way to animate every visual change consistently.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Transition only deliberate properties with measured timing; avoid transition:all and keep state understandable without motion.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: transition: all is the safest way to animate every visual change consistently.
misconceptions.correction: Transition only deliberate properties with measured timing; avoid transition:all and keep state understandable without motion.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: transition: all is the safest way to animate every visual change consistently.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Transforms, transitions and animations, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Transforms, transitions and animations before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement purposeful state motion and explain its start, end and interruption behavior. Keep this boundary in view: Display does not generally interpolate like opacity, layout properties can be expensive, and motion must not hide state or block interaction.
